title: 2MASS

infobox.value: * [Digitized Sky Survey](/wiki/Digitized_Sky_Survey "Digitized Sky Survey") (DSS) * [The INT Photometric H-Alpha Survey](/wiki/The_INT_Photometric_H-Alpha_Survey "The INT Photometric H-Alpha Survey") (IPHAS) * [NGS-POSS](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society_%E2%80%93_Palomar_Observatory_Sky_Survey "National Geographic Society – Palomar Observatory Sky Survey") * [Palomar Distant Solar System Survey](/wiki/Palomar_Distant_Solar_System_Survey "Palomar Distant Solar System Survey") (PDSSS) * [SLUGGS](/wiki/SAGES_Legacy_Unifying_Globulars_and_GalaxieS_Survey "SAGES Legacy Unifying Globulars and GalaxieS Survey")

* 2MASS * [Akari (Astro-F)](/wiki/Akari_(satellite) "Akari (satellite)") * [Infrared Astronomical Satellite](/wiki/IRAS "IRAS") (IRAS) * [UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey](/wiki/UKIRT_Infrared_Deep_Sky_Survey "UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey") (UKIDSS) * [SCUBA-2 All Sky Survey](/wiki/Submillimetre_Common-User_Bolometer_Array#SCUBA-2_All_Sky_Survey "Submillimetre Common-User Bolometer Array") * [VISTA](/wiki/VISTA_(telescope) "VISTA (telescope)") * [Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer](/wiki/Wide-field_Infrared_Survey_Explorer "Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer") (WISE)

* [C-BASS](/wiki/C-Band_All_Sky_Survey "C-Band All Sky Survey") * [GALEX Arecibo SDSS Survey](/wiki/GALEX_Arecibo_SDSS_Survey "GALEX Arecibo SDSS Survey") (GASS) * [HIPASS](/wiki/HIPASS "HIPASS") * [NVSS](/wiki/NRAO_VLA_Sky_Survey "NRAO VLA Sky Survey") * [Ohio Sky Survey](/wiki/Ohio_Sky_Survey "Ohio Sky Survey") * [PALFA Survey](/wiki/PALFA_Survey "PALFA Survey")

* [2dF Galaxy Redshift Survey](/wiki/2dF_Galaxy_Redshift_Survey "2dF Galaxy Redshift Survey") (2dFGRS) * [6dF Galaxy Survey](/wiki/6dF_Galaxy_Survey "6dF Galaxy Survey") (6dFGS) * [Calar Alto Legacy Integral Field Area Survey](/wiki/Calar_Alto_Legacy_Integral_Field_Area_Survey "Calar Alto Legacy Integral Field Area Survey")  (CALIFA) * [CfA Redshift Survey](/wiki/CfA_Redshift_Survey "CfA Redshift Survey") * [Dark Energy Survey](/wiki/Dark_Energy_Survey "Dark Energy Survey") (DES) * [DEEP2 Redshift Survey](/wiki/DEEP2_Redshift_Survey "DEEP2 Redshift Survey") (DEEP2) * [Galaxy And Mass Assembly survey](/wiki/Galaxy_And_Mass_Assembly_survey "Galaxy And Mass Assembly survey") (GAMA) * [LAMOST](/wiki/LAMOST "LAMOST") * [Las Campanas Redshift Survey](/wiki/Las_Campanas_Redshift_Survey "Las Campanas Redshift Survey") * [Sloan Digital Sky Survey](/wiki/Sloan_Digital_Sky_Survey "Sloan Digital Sky Survey") (SDSS) * [VIMOS-VLT Deep Survey](/wiki/VIMOS-VLT_Deep_Survey "VIMOS-VLT Deep Survey") (VVDS) * [WiggleZ](/wiki/WiggleZ_Dark_Energy_Survey "WiggleZ Dark Energy Survey")

was an [astronomical survey](/wiki/Astronomical_survey "Astronomical survey") of the whole sky in [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") light.[[1]](#cite_note-about-1) It took place between 1997 and 2001, in two different locations: at the U.S. [Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory](/wiki/Fred_Lawrence_Whipple_Observatory "Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory") on Mount Hopkins, Arizona, and at the [Cerro Tololo Inter-American Observatory](/wiki/Cerro_Tololo_Inter-American_Observatory "Cerro Tololo Inter-American Observatory") in Chile, each using a 1.3-meter telescope for the [Northern and Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Hemispheres_of_the_Earth "Hemispheres of the Earth"), respectively.[[2]](#cite_note-archive-2) It was conducted in the [short-wavelength infrared](/wiki/Short-wavelength_infrared "Short-wavelength infrared") at three distinct [frequency bands](/wiki/Frequency_band "Frequency band") ([J](/wiki/J_band_(infrared) "J band (infrared)"), [H](/wiki/H_band_(infrared) "H band (infrared)"), and [K](/wiki/K_band_(infrared) "K band (infrared)")) near 2 [micrometres](/wiki/Micrometre "Micrometre"), from which the [photometric](/wiki/Photometry_(astronomy) "Photometry (astronomy)") survey with its [HgCdTe](/wiki/Mercury_cadmium_telluride "Mercury cadmium telluride") detectors derives its name.[[1]](#cite_note-about-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Astronomical survey of the whole sky in the infrared

2MASS

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Alternative names | Two Micron All-Sky Survey |
| Website | [www.ipac.caltech.edu/2mass/](http://www.ipac.caltech.edu/2mass/) |
| [Related media on Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:2MASS "commons:Category:2MASS") | |
| [[edit on Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q1454942 "d:Q1454942")] | |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/56/2MASS_J17111353%2B2326333.jpg)](/wiki/File:2MASS_J17111353%2B2326333.jpg)

2MASS [J-band](/wiki/J_band_(infrared) "J band (infrared)") image. The [brown dwarf](/wiki/Brown_dwarf "Brown dwarf") [2MASS J17111353+2326333](/w/index.php?title=2MASS_J17111353%2B2326333&action=edit&redlink=1 "2MASS J17111353+2326333 (page does not exist)") is highlighted.

The **Two Micron All-Sky Survey (2MASS)** was an [astronomical survey](/wiki/Astronomical_survey "Astronomical survey") of the whole sky in [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") light.[[1]](#cite_note-about-1) It took place between 1997 and 2001, in two different locations: at the U.S. [Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory](/wiki/Fred_Lawrence_Whipple_Observatory "Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory") on Mount Hopkins, Arizona, and at the [Cerro Tololo Inter-American Observatory](/wiki/Cerro_Tololo_Inter-American_Observatory "Cerro Tololo Inter-American Observatory") in Chile, each using a 1.3-meter telescope for the [Northern and Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Hemispheres_of_the_Earth "Hemispheres of the Earth"), respectively.[[2]](#cite_note-archive-2) It was conducted in the [short-wavelength infrared](/wiki/Short-wavelength_infrared "Short-wavelength infrared") at three distinct [frequency bands](/wiki/Frequency_band "Frequency band") ([J](/wiki/J_band_(infrared) "J band (infrared)"), [H](/wiki/H_band_(infrared) "H band (infrared)"), and [K](/wiki/K_band_(infrared) "K band (infrared)")) near 2 [micrometres](/wiki/Micrometre "Micrometre"), from which the [photometric](/wiki/Photometry_(astronomy) "Photometry (astronomy)") survey with its [HgCdTe](/wiki/Mercury_cadmium_telluride "Mercury cadmium telluride") detectors derives its name.[[1]](#cite_note-about-1)

2MASS produced an [astronomical catalog](/wiki/Astronomical_catalog "Astronomical catalog") with over 300 million observed [objects](/wiki/Astronomical_objects "Astronomical objects"), including [minor planets](/wiki/Minor_planet "Minor planet") of the Solar System, [brown dwarfs](/wiki/Brown_dwarf "Brown dwarf"), [low-mass stars](/wiki/Low-mass_stars "Low-mass stars"), [nebulae](/wiki/Nebula "Nebula"), [star clusters](/wiki/Star_cluster "Star cluster") and [galaxies](/wiki/Galaxies "Galaxies"). In addition, 1 million objects were cataloged in the *2MASS Extended Source Catalog* (*2MASX*). The cataloged objects are designated with a "2MASS" and "2MASX" prefix, respectively.

## Catalog

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2MASS&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Catalog")]

The final data release for 2MASS occurred in 2003,[[3]](#cite_note-2MASS_FAQ-3) and is served by the [Infrared Science Archive](/wiki/Infrared_Science_Archive "Infrared Science Archive"). The goals of this survey included:

* Detection of galaxies in the "[Zone of Avoidance](/wiki/Zone_of_Avoidance "Zone of Avoidance")", a strip of sky obscured in visible light by our own galaxy, the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way").
* Detection of brown dwarfs. 2MASS discovered a total of 173, including [2MASS 0939-2448](/wiki/2MASS_0939-2448 "2MASS 0939-2448"), [2MASS 0415-0935](/wiki/2MASS_0415-0935 "2MASS 0415-0935"), [2M1207](/wiki/2M1207 "2M1207"), and [2MASS J04414489+2301513](/wiki/2MASS_J04414489%2B2301513 "2MASS J04414489+2301513").[[4]](#cite_note-Kirkpatrick2003-4)
* An extensive survey of low mass stars, the most common type of star both in our own galaxy and others.
* Cataloging of all detected stars and galaxies.
* Infrared measurements from the 2MASS survey have been particularly effective at unveiling previously undiscovered star clusters.[[5]](#cite_note-fr2007-5)[[6]](#cite_note-ma2013-6)

Numerical descriptions of point sources ([stars](/wiki/Star "Star"), [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet"), [asteroids](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid")) and extended sources (galaxies, nebulae) were cataloged by automated computer programs to an average limiting [magnitude](/wiki/Apparent_magnitude "Apparent magnitude") of about 14. More than 300 million [point sources](/wiki/Point_source "Point source") and 1 million extended sources were cataloged. In November 2003, a team of scientists announced the discovery of the [Canis Major Dwarf Galaxy](/wiki/Canis_Major_Dwarf_Galaxy "Canis Major Dwarf Galaxy"), at that time the closest known satellite galaxy to the Milky Way, based on analysis of 2MASS stellar data.

The resulting data and images from the survey are currently in the [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain"), and may be accessed online for free by anyone.[[7]](#cite_note-7) There is also a list of 2MASS science publications with links to free pre-publication copies of the papers.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

2MASS is sponsored by the [University of Massachusetts Amherst](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst"), the [Infrared Processing and Analysis Center](/wiki/Infrared_Processing_and_Analysis_Center "Infrared Processing and Analysis Center") (IPAC, run by [Jet Propulsion Laboratory](/wiki/Jet_Propulsion_Laboratory "Jet Propulsion Laboratory") (JPL) and [Caltech](/wiki/Caltech "Caltech")), [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA"), and the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation") (NSF).
